text: After inserting the VATS camera, we were able to better visualize the extent of the intrathoracic invasion.
Both the fourth and fifth rib were completely encased in the tumor and would have to be resected, but it did appear that we'd be able to resect the whole tumor and both ribs en bloc.
First, the tumor was located externally by elevating the trapezius and latissimus dorsi muscles.
The tumor was dissected with a small cuff of skeletal muscle still attached in order to ensure that no involved tissue remained.
Here you can see how the tumor extends far beyond the length of the incision toward the apex of the chest.
We then use the VATS camera to guide us as we separated the tissue from the inferior aspect of the fifth rib.
Ribs four and five were cut along the lateral border of the tumor closer to the spine, and then the intercostal musculature was cauterized to free the posterior aspect of the tumor.
We then moved Antero medially and cut through ribs 4 and 5 along the medial border of the tumor.
The intercostal musculature between ribs 3 and 4 was then cauterized until finally the entire tumor could be removed on block.
The right arm was mobilized and it was discovered that the inferior angle of the scapula easily slipped into the chest wall defect, so the tip was resected to prevent entrapment.
Once again, using the VATS camera to guide the reconstruction, we inserted a Gore-Tex patch measuring 18 centimeters by 24 centimeters by one millimeter.
The Gore-Tex patch was attached to the chest wall using a protacker.
The mesh was then assessed for approximation to the chest wall, and the remaining border was secured with suture.
The lung was then slowly inflated, and we assessed the completed reconstruction externally.
The incisions were then closed in layers.
A 24 French axiom drain was placed in the intrapleural space, and a 10 French JP drain was placed in the extrapleural space.
